--- original
+++ modified
@@ -0,0 +1,73 @@
+---
+assignees:
+- claude-code
+position_column: todo
+position_ordinal: cc80
+title: 'Perf: task.move reads all tasks on every drop — pass before_id+after_id from frontend zones, target <300ms end-to-end on 2000-task board'
+---
+## What
+
+Dragging and dropping a task on a board with 2000 cards takes seconds; the target is <300ms end-to-end so it feels human-instant.
+
+### Root cause (primary hot path)
+
+`swissarmyhammer-kanban/src/task/mv.rs:113` (and the symmetric branch at `:219`):
+
+```rust
+let all_tasks = ectx.list("task").await?;
+let mut col_tasks: Vec<_> = all_tasks.into_iter()
+    .filter(|t| t.get_str("position_column") == Some(self.column.as_str())
+        && t.id.as_str() != self.id.as_str())
+    .collect();
+col_tasks.sort_by(...);
+// then .position(|t| t.id == ref_id) to locate the anchor
+```
+
+Every drag-drop triggers `ectx.list("task")`, which is implemented by `swissarmyhammer-entity/src/io.rs:145 read_entity_dir` → one concurrent file read + YAML parse per task. For 2000 files this is tens-to-hundreds of ms of disk I/O plus parsing, for a logical operation whose work only touches the moved task and 1-2 neighbor tasks.
+
+The rest of the pipeline (store patch, `useBoardLayout` sort, virtualized render) is much cheaper and not what makes it "take seconds".
+
+### Why the frontend already knows everything we need
+
+`kanban-app/ui/src/lib/drop-zones.ts:31-49` — the UI already computes drop zones against the sorted column and knows **both** neighbors for every in-between zone. But today `DropZoneDescriptor` only carries one anchor (`beforeId` XOR `afterId`), so the backend has to rediscover the missing neighbor by listing and sorting the entire task collection.
+
+The backend's `Ordinal` helper already handles both-neighbors correctly: `swissarmyhammer-kanban/src/task_helpers.rs:83-90 compute_ordinal_for_neighbors(before, after)` → `Ordinal::between(before, after)` when both are given.
+
+### Fix
+
+Trust the frontend's sorted column view. Have the drop zone carry both anchors. Backend reads the 0-2 named tasks directly, computes the ordinal, done — no `list("task")` on the drag path.
+
+Subtasks:
+
+- [ ] Extend `DropZoneDescriptor` (`kanban-app/ui/src/lib/drop-zones.ts:10-19`) and `computeDropZones` (`:31-49`) so that each in-between zone carries **both** `beforeId` (the card the new task will land above) and `afterId` (the card immediately above the zone). First zone: only `beforeId`. Last zone: only `afterId`. Empty-column zone: neither.
+- [ ] Update `persistMove` in `kanban-app/ui/src/components/board-view.tsx:333-353` to forward `descriptor.beforeId` and `descriptor.afterId` both when present (the current code already reads both from descriptor; verify nothing strips one of them before dispatch).
+- [ ] Rewrite the `before_id`/`after_id` branch of `MoveTask::execute` (`swissarmyhammer-kanban/src/task/mv.rs:111-217`) to skip `ectx.list("task")` entirely: read the 1–2 named anchor tasks via `ectx.read("task", id)`, extract their `position_ordinal`, and call `compute_ordinal_for_neighbors(before, after)`. Leave the "neither given" branch (`:218-244`) alone — non-drag callers (MCP, CLI) can still append-at-end via the current list-based path.
+- [ ] Update the Rust tests that exercise `with_before`/`with_after` in `swissarmyhammer-kanban/src/task/mv.rs:348-430` to also cover the "both anchors given" case that drag-drop now emits, and verify the "anchor not found" error surface is unchanged (or explicitly document the new behavior: missing anchor returns an error instead of silently appending, since the frontend should never send a stale id).
+- [ ] Add a targeted Criterion (or similar) benchmark in `swissarmyhammer-kanban/benches/` that seeds a board with 2000 tasks and measures a single `MoveTask::execute` with `before_id`+`after_id`. Assert wall-clock <50ms on CI hardware — leaving ~250ms headroom for the IPC round-trip, event fan-out, and React render to stay under the 300ms feel-instant budget.
+- [ ] Add a frontend unit test in `kanban-app/ui/src/lib/drop-zones.test.ts` proving that `computeDropZones(["a","b","c"], "todo")` yields the expected five zones with the right `(beforeId, afterId)` pairs, including the end-cases.
+
+### Out of scope (file follow-up cards if needed)
+
+- React-side claimWhen predicate computation in `kanban-app/ui/src/components/column-view.tsx:299-377` is O(tasks_in_col × tasks_in_adjacent_col). With 500 cards/col this is 250K iterations per column-recompute. Not the drag bottleneck today, but worth watching once the backend no longer dominates.
+- Optimistic UI update in `persistMove` (moving the card locally before the backend confirms) would cut perceived latency further but changes the correctness model (conflict handling on rejection). Separate concern.
+- Append-at-end branch (`mv.rs:218-244`) still does a full list. Only hit by non-drag callers; address only if benchmarks show it in a hot path.
+
+## Acceptance Criteria
+
+- [ ] `MoveTask::execute` with `before_id` and/or `after_id` provided does not call `ectx.list("task")` on its fast path (verified by a unit test that counts calls via a test double, or by inspecting the implementation diff).
+- [ ] On a seeded 2000-task board, a single `MoveTask` with both anchors runs in <50ms on CI in the new benchmark.
+- [ ] End-to-end: in the running kanban-app, dragging a card to a new slot on a 2000-task board visibly lands the card in <300ms measured from drop event to UI update (manual check via performance trace; note the measured number in the PR description).
+- [ ] `DropZoneDescriptor` now carries both `beforeId` and `afterId` for interior zones; `computeDropZones` populates them; `persistMove` forwards both.
+- [ ] All existing drag-drop tests continue to pass — `kanban-app/ui/src/components/board-integration.browser.test.tsx` and any Rust integration tests for move.
+
+## Tests
+
+- [ ] `swissarmyhammer-kanban/src/task/mv.rs` — add `test_move_task_before_and_after_skips_list`: seed 3 tasks in column, call `MoveTask::to_column(c, "todo").with_before(b).with_after(a)`, assert the resulting ordinal is strictly between A's and B's ordinals. Verify no full list by instrumenting `EntityContext` with a read-counter test double OR by asserting only the anchor ids appear in a trace.
+- [ ] `swissarmyhammer-kanban/benches/move_task_bench.rs` (new) — Criterion bench seeding 2000 tasks distributed across 4 columns, moving one between two anchors. `cargo bench -p swissarmyhammer-kanban --bench move_task_bench` prints median <50ms.
+- [ ] `kanban-app/ui/src/lib/drop-zones.test.ts` — assert `computeDropZones` emits zones with `{beforeId: "a"}`, `{afterId: "a", beforeId: "b"}`, `{afterId: "b", beforeId: "c"}`, `{afterId: "c"}` for `["a","b","c"]`, and `{}` for `[]`.
+- [ ] `cargo nextest run -p swissarmyhammer-kanban -- move_task` — existing suite still green.
+- [ ] `cd kanban-app/ui && bun run test drop-zones` — passes including new cases.
+- [ ] Manual regression: build the app, load a 2000-task board (seed via `sah kanban` or a test fixture), drag a card between two others, record a Chrome DevTools performance trace, confirm total drop-to-paint <300ms.
+
+## Workflow
+- Use `/tdd` — start by writing the Rust unit test that asserts `list("task")` is not called on the before/after path (this fails immediately against the current implementation), then the frontend `computeDropZones` test, then rewrite the backend branch to make them both pass.

-### Why the frontend already knows everything we need
+`swissarmyhammer-entity/src/cache.rs` defines `EntityCache`, an in-memory store keyed by `(entity_type, id)` with:
+- `load_all(entity_type)` — bulk-populate from disk on startup (`cache.rs:100`)
+- `get(type, id)` / `get_all(type)` — O(1) reads, no disk (`cache.rs:122`, `:137`)
+- `write(entity)` — writes through to `EntityContext::write` and updates the cache in lock-step, bumping version + emitting `EntityEvent::EntityChanged` (`cache.rs:152`)
+- `refresh_from_disk` / `evict` — points the file watcher already calls on external changes (`cache.rs:228, 244`)
+- `EntityWatcher` in `swissarmyhammer-entity/src/watcher.rs:33` — wraps an `Arc<EntityCache>` and routes fs-notify events through it

-`kanban-app/ui/src/lib/drop-zones.ts:31-49` — the UI already computes drop zones against the sorted column and knows **both** neighbors for every in-between zone. But today `DropZoneDescriptor` only carries one anchor (`beforeId` XOR `afterId`), so the backend has to rediscover the missing neighbor by listing and sorting the entire task collection.
+Everything for "cache all entities in memory, refresh on write or file change" is **implemented** in the entity crate. It just is not plugged into the kanban data path.

-The backend's `Ordinal` helper already handles both-neighbors correctly: `swissarmyhammer-kanban/src/task_helpers.rs:83-90 compute_ordinal_for_neighbors(before, after)` → `Ordinal::between(before, after)` when both are given.
